identity.definition_origin: Codified by Rauno Freiberg (Staff Design Engineer, Vercel; ex–The Browser Company) in the essay 'Invisible Details of Interaction Design' (July 2023) and the Devouring Details course/community (2024-2025, devouringdetails.com). Earlier lineage: Brian Lovin's 'Design Details' / 'App Dissection' series (2014-), Karri Saarinen's craft writing and Linear's 'building with taste' doctrine (Lenny's Newsletter 2024, First Round podcast 2024), Adam Wathan & Steve Schoger's 'Refactoring UI' (2018). The shared thesis: distinction is achieved through accumulated micro-decisions in typography, motion, and interaction, not through novel layouts.
identity.core_mechanism: Distinctive design moves exploit the human attentional system's sensitivity to anomaly within an otherwise familiar pattern. When 95% of an interface follows convention and 5% breaks it in a considered way (an off-grid headline, a custom spring on one element, a non-default cursor, a 'weird-but-considered' detail), the brain's prediction-error response flags the deviation and tags the product as 'made by someone'. This is the same mechanism Don Norman calls 'visceral plus reflective' — visceral pleasure from polish, reflective pleasure from noticing the choice. Karri Saarinen calls it 'the things that make you smile' and explicitly checks for them at ship time.
identity.neuroscience_correlate: [uncertain] Plausibly mesolimbic dopamine on novelty / reward-prediction error, plus default-mode-network engagement when the user notices and reflects on a craft detail. Not directly measured in the cited craft literature.

evidence.empirical_evidence: Largely qualitative / industry-cred evidence. (1) Devouring Details ($249, 23 chapters, 23 React components) earned an Awwwards Honorable Mention and One Page Love award; endorsed by heads of design at multiple top product companies. (2) Rauno's open-source cmdk library (the Linear / Vercel command-bar engine) is downloaded millions of times per week per the Devouring Details positioning copy. (3) Karri Saarinen's craft doctrine is credited inside Linear with their distinctive market position vs. Jira/Asana — Lenny's Newsletter and First Round podcast both attribute Linear's growth-through-design-quality to ship-time animation review. (4) Brian Lovin's 'Design Details' series has run since 2014 dissecting Stripe iOS, Shorts, etc.; cited by product designers as the canonical reference. (5) Refactoring UI (Wathan/Schoger 2018) sold >100k copies. No controlled study isolates 'distinctive moves' from baseline UX, but A/B-testable signals (NPS lift, retention from delight) are reported anecdotally by Linear and Stripe.
evidence.measurable_outcome_metric: NPS, unaided brand recall, 'made by humans' blinded rater score, D30 retention delta on builds with vs. without polish pass, social shares of a single screen / animation (Twitter virality), and qualitative craft praise volume in design discourse.

application.engineering_tactics: Specific moves drawn from Rauno Freiberg, Karri Saarinen, Brian Lovin, Refactoring UI: (1) Opinionated typography — pick one decisive type pair (e.g., Söhne + Tiempos at Stripe; GT-America-adjacent + Berkeley Mono at Linear; Cabinet Grotesk + Manrope as anti-slop default); use 45-75 chars/line per Refactoring UI; weight contrast over color contrast for hierarchy. (2) Asymmetric grids — break the 12-col grid intentionally on hero, leave one column under-populated. (3) Restrained palette + one bold accent — Linear's purple, Mercury's burnt orange, Things' blue checkmark are the entire brand. (4) Custom motion personality — every product has its own easing curve. Linear cmd-K uses a backdrop blur reveal at ~150ms with custom cubic-bezier; Things 3 ships custom check animation with paired iOS haptic [uncertain on exact 320ms timing]; Arc spaces sidebar slides at 0.2s ease-out 240px→48px collapse with 0.15s ease-in opacity at 0.1s delay (Blake Crosley reverse-engineering). Vercel uses Framer Motion springs at stiffness ~300 / damping ~30. (5) 'Weird-but-considered' details — Vercel's geometric loading triangle, Linear's keyboard-shortcut hints baked into hover, Arc's Easel mode, Stripe's ripple-on-touch on iOS Dashboard (Brian Lovin). (6) Custom illustration system rather than stock or AI-gen. (7) Hand-tuned focus rings, custom selection colors, custom cursors on web. (8) Ship-time review: Karri's rule — if an animation doesn't feel right, it doesn't ship.
application.real_world_examples: (1) Linear command bar (cmd-K) — ~60% screen-width modal with backdrop blur, custom keyboard navigation showing inline shortcut hints, fade+scale entrance animation; powered by Rauno's open-source cmdk library. (2) Things 3 check animation — animated blue checkmark draw paired with subtle iOS haptic on completion; the app uses a 'custom-built animation toolkit' per Cultured Code's own marketing. (3) Arc spaces sidebar transition — width transitions 240px→48px with `width 0.2s ease-out`, text opacity 0.15s ease-in with 0.1s delay; Spaces themes shift on switch with custom color cross-fade. (4) Vercel marketing footer / homepage (rauno.me/craft/vercel) — the 'ship' word animates with bespoke spring; geometric loading triangle is signature. (5) Stripe Dashboard for iOS (Brian Lovin App Dissection) — graph scrubbing reveals/hides info to avoid clunky tooltips; subtle bouncing on data points; Material-style ripple on touch ported to iOS. (6) Things 3 magic-plus button — drag-to-reorder with custom physics + haptic, breaking iOS conventions in a beloved way. (7) Raycast — custom illustration system across marketing, opinionated keyboard-first in-product palette.
application.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile distinctive moves lean on haptic + spring physics + gesture (Things 3 check, Arc Mobile's custom navigation, iOS swipe coupled to UIImpactFeedbackGenerator). Web distinctive moves lean on cursor + hover + keyboard shortcuts + scroll-coupled motion (Linear cmd-K, Vercel hover triangles, rauno.me scroll-driven hero). Web also has more typography surface area; mobile leans on iconography and rhythm.
application.platform_design_language_alignment: Selectively breaks platform DLs at marketing surfaces and high-signature moments while honoring HIG / Material 3 Expressive primitives for accessibility (focus, hit targets, dynamic type). Linear, Things, Arc all over-customize chrome; Mercury and Stripe customize typography but keep iOS modal/sheet primitives. Generally fights Liquid Glass / Material 3 defaults at marketing-page level, conforms at OS-component level.

ai_workflow.claude_design_application: Inject named references and exact motion specs into the prompt. Example fragment 1: 'Build a command bar following the Linear cmd-K pattern: 60% screen-width modal at desktop, full-width on mobile, backdrop with backdrop-filter: blur(12px), 150ms fade+scale-in (initial scale 0.96, easing cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1)), inline keyboard-shortcut hints on hover, fuzzy search via the cmdk library, esc-to-close, focus-trap inside.' Example fragment 2: 'Add one weird-but-considered detail: when the user completes the primary CTA, animate a custom checkmark draw over 320ms using stroke-dashoffset, paired with navigator.vibrate(20) on Android and a UIImpactFeedbackGenerator-equivalent in Capacitor. Forbid generic Lucide check icon.' Always specify named easing curves and durations rather than 'smooth'.
ai_workflow.stitch_application: Stitch needs explicit visual vocabulary because it cannot see references. Example fragment 1: 'Type pair: GT America Mono Bold for headline at 56px / -1% tracking + Inter Display Regular for body at 17px / 1.5 line-height — but flag we are NOT using default Inter, this is the Display variant. Brand accent: single saturated near-violet #5E6AD2 (Linear-style) used only on primary CTA and active state. All other UI in neutral grays #FAFAFA / #1A1A1A. Asymmetric 12-column grid — headline spans cols 1-7, supporting visual cols 8-12.' Example fragment 2: 'Add a signature detail: a small custom geometric mark (triangle inscribed in circle) in the bottom-left corner that subtly rotates on page-load. This is the brand fingerprint. No icon library. No stock illustration.' Forbid: shadcn cards, Lucide icons, purple gradients, centered everything.
ai_workflow.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen butchers distinctive moves in three ways: (1) It defaults to Lucide / Heroicons for any glyph and cannot invent a custom mark — counter: 'No icon libraries. Generate inline SVG with these specific path commands [...] or describe a mark to commission.' (2) It uses generic ease-in-out at 200ms for everything — counter: 'All motion uses cubic-bezier(0.32, 0.72, 0, 1) at 220-320ms unless explicitly overridden. Spring motion uses Framer Motion useSpring with stiffness 300 / damping 30.' (3) It collapses to Tailwind defaults (rounded-lg, shadow-sm, gray-200) — counter: 'No Tailwind defaults. Define custom CSS variables for radii (4px, 10px, 16px), shadows (use 0 1px 0 + 0 8px 24px stacked), and a 5-step neutral ramp. Reject any class that ends in -200/300/400.' Final guard: 'Before returning, name three details that make this design feel non-generic. If you cannot, redesign.'

risks.anti_patterns: (1) Detail theater — applying micro-interactions to every element until everything moves and nothing reads (motion-for-its-own-sake, #10 on Creative Boom's 'so over' list). (2) Cargo-culting Linear/Stripe's specific palette without their underlying systems thinking. (3) Custom cursor / animations that break accessibility, focus management, prefers-reduced-motion. (4) 'Weird detail' that is just weird, not considered — without semantic justification it reads as random. (5) Spending the polish budget on marketing pages while in-product UX rots. (6) Ignoring performance: hand-tuned springs and Lottie animations that blow INP/LCP.
risks.regulatory_risk: Low. Craft details are aesthetic. Edge case: Medium if motion ignores prefers-reduced-motion (EU EAA accessibility, US ADA exposure for vestibular-disorder triggers).
risks.pitfalls_caveats: Performance cost of hand-tuned animations and custom fonts; A11y exposure (custom focus rings, reduced-motion support); requires senior design + engineering talent — distinctive moves are expensive to produce and maintain; risk of looking dated in 2-3 years (Liquid Glass will date faster than Helvetica). Cultural specificity: 'craft' as a marketing claim resonates in NA/EU but may read as fussy in markets where speed-to-feature is the trust signal.
risks.cross_cultural_validity: Unverified. The Devouring Details / craft canon is North-Atlantic and English-language-first. Examples lean Anglo (Linear, Stripe, Vercel, Arc, Things). Japanese craft tradition (Cultured Code is German but channels Japanese ma / restraint) maps well; CN super-app maximalism does not. RTL: most cited examples have not been audited for RTL distinctive-move equivalence.

sources: [Devouring Details — Rauno Freiberg's interaction-design reference manual](https://devouringdetails.com/)
[Brian Lovin — Stripe Dashboard for iOS App Dissection](https://brianlovin.com/design-details/stripe-dashboard-ios)
[Karri Saarinen — Inside Linear: Building with taste, craft, and focus (Lenny's Newsletter)](https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/inside-linear-building-with-taste)
[Karri Saarinen — 10 Rules for Crafting Products That Stand Out (Figma Blog)](https://www.figma.com/blog/karri-saarinens-10-rules-for-crafting-products-that-stand-out/)
[Blake Crosley — Arc Browser: Reimagining the Browser Chrome](https://blakecrosley.com/guides/design/arc)
